segments.s117-g145: فرستاد is the past tense, 3rd-person singular of فرستادن (“he sent”). نامه is the direct object that follows the verb here. به نزدیک اوی is a prepositional phrase marking direction/recipient: به = 'to', نزدیک functions as a noun 'the close one/intimate', and اوی is the archaic/enclitic possessive/pronoun meaning 'his' (literally 'to his near one').
segments.s117-g146: برافروختن is the infinitival/verbal-noun form (masdar) meaning 'to kindle/ inflame' and here expresses purpose — why the letter was sent (equivalent to 'in order to...'). جان تاریک اوی is a genitival/attributive chain: جان 'soul' modified by the adjective تاریک 'dark', followed by the possessive/enclitic اوی 'his' (the soul belonging to him).
segments.s117-g33: به کردار دوزخ is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning “in the manner/likeness of Hell” that modifies the following noun. یکی غار is an indefinite noun phrase “a cave,” and دید is the simple past verb “saw/beheld.” The sentence omits an explicit subject (classical Persian often leaves the 3rd‑person subject implicit), so the structure is: adverbial phrase + indefinite NP + past verb.
segments.s117-g34: تن دیو is a noun phrase “the body of the demon” (possession by juxtaposition). از تیرگی is a locative/ablative phrase “out of/from the darkness,” and ناپدید functions as the verbal/adjectival predicate “vanished/was gone” (classical style often uses the bare participle/adjective where Modern Persian would use شدن). Word order: subject phrase → prepositional phrase → predicate.
segments.s117-g35: زمانی همی بود is a temporal+continuous past construction: زمانی “for a while,” همی (an older particle) gives durative sense, and بود is the past of “to be.” در چنگ تیغ is a figurative locative phrase “in the grip/hand (claw) of the sword,” placed after the verb to describe his condition. The implied subject is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s117-g36: نبد is an archaic negative existential (here “there was not / did not exist”) preceding the nouns it negates. جای دیدار و راه گریغ are two coordinated nominal phrases (“place of meeting” and “way/path of …”), joined by و; نبد negates both. Word order is negative‑verb + noun phrases (poetic; English renders it as “there was no … and no …”).
segments.s117-g37: ازان تیرگی is an ablative/locative phrase “from that darkness.” دیده here is the noun “the eye” (subject), and ندید is the negated past verb “did not see.” Classical Persian often uses the concrete noun “eye” as the grammatical subject before the verb. Word order: prepositional phrase → subject → verb.
segments.s117-g38: زمانی is an adverbial “for a while.” بران is a locative/relational particle “upon that/thereon,” and جایگه is the noun “place/spot.” آرمید is the simple past verb “rested” or “lay down.” The subject is again omitted and understood from prior clauses; sequence is temporal adverb → locative phrase → verb.
segments.s117-g39: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause “when.” مژگان بمالید is a past action “(he) rubbed his eyelashes” (subject omitted). و coordinates the two actions; دیده بشست is “(he) washed his eyes,” where بشست is the past form with the older prefix b‑ (classical conjugation). Both verbs share the same implicit subject.
segments.s117-g40: دران جای تاریک is a locative phrase “in that dark place” (دران = در + ان = “in that”). لختی is an adverb “for a short while,” placed before the verb for emphasis, and بجست is a past tense verb meaning “sought/looked about/searched.” The subject remains the same implicit actor from the previous clause; order is locative → adverbial → verb.
segments.s117-g9: A temporal concessive clause: کنون 'now' opens the clause; چون گه رفتن آمد فراز is an archaic word order meaning 'when the time (occasion) of going to the heights comes'. رفتن is the verbal noun 'going' followed by آمد 'came/comes' to make the temporal sense 'when ... comes'. فراز (literally 'on high') gives the destination/scene.
segments.s117-g10: Imperative requests directed to the addressee. مرا+را functions as the object 'me' (م + را split in the word list); راه بنمای is an imperative 'show the way' (بنمای = show/reveal). و links two imperatives; بگشای راز is another imperative 'uncover/reveal the secret'.
segments.s117-g11: Simple reported-speech frame: بدو is the dative/clitic 'to him', گفت is the past verb 'said' (here treated as the speech verb), and اولاد is the speaker 'the son/child'. چون آفتاب begins the subordinate temporal clause 'when the sun...'. Word order places the indirect object (بدو) before the reporting verb.
segments.s117-g12: The temporal clause predicate: شود گرم = 'becomes hot' (شود as present/future/become). و links the second clause: دیو اندر آید به خواب = 'the demon enters into sleep' (آید 'comes' + به خواب 'into sleep' = 'falls asleep'). Note the stative/resultative sense of آید here.
segments.s117-g13: A fronted prepositional phrase gives the adversary: بریشان 'against/over them' (بر + ایشان compressed). تو is the subject 'you', پیروز 'victorious' is predicate adjective, and باشی is the present/subjunctive form of 'to be' used here as a wishing/predictive locution ('may you be' / 'you will be'). به جنگ 'in battle' marks the field where victory applies.
segments.s117-g14: Statement of necessity with inverted order: کنون 'now' + یک زمان 'for one moment/one while' followed by کرد (light verb 'do/make') and باید 'it is necessary' and the noun درنگ 'a pause/tarrying'. Classical Persian often places باید before or after the infinitive/light-verb construction; the sense is 'now a short delay must be made' → 'you must pause a while'.
segments.s117-g15: Negative existential/observational clause: ز 'from/among' + دیوان (here 'the divs/host of supernatural beings') is the domain; نبینی is the negative present/subjunctive 'you will not see', and نشسته یکی 'one seated' places the quantified noun after the participle. Word order yields 'you will not see one sitting among the divs.'
segments.s117-g16: Exception clause: جز از 'except from/among' + جادوان 'the enchanters/magicians' names the group; پاسبان 'guards' is the role, and اندکی 'a few' quantifies them. Together: 'except for a few guards among the magicians.'
segments.s117-g49: برآشفت is a past-tense/perfective verb meaning 'became angry/sprang up' and here describes the elephant's sudden agitation. پیل ژیان is a noun phrase 'the living/animated elephant' (پیل = elephant, ژیان = living) acting as the subject; برسان appears in apposition to the noun phrase and is an older/poetic form that here helps qualify or introduce the elephant (conveying the sense of 'the said/brought-forward'), so the clause reads roughly 'the living elephant sprang up.'
segments.s117-g50: یکی is an indefinite pronoun 'one/someone' (here 'someone'); تیغ تیزش is a noun phrase 'his sharp sword' (تیغ = sword, تیزش = 'his sharp' where -ش is the enclitic possessive 'his'). بزد is a simple past 'he struck'. بر میان is a prepositional phrase 'at/in the middle' (بر = against/on, میان = middle). The clause as a whole: 'someone struck his sharp sword at the middle.'
segments.s117-g51: ز نیروی رستم is an ablative/agentive phrase 'by/from the strength of Rostam' (ز = from/by, نیروی = the strength/power of, رستم = Rostam). ز بالای اوی repeats the ablative/location sense 'from above him' (بالای = above, اوی = 'him' with an archaic/poetic enclitic). Together they set the cause and point of attack: 'by Rostam's power, from above him...'.
segments.s117-g52: بینداخت is a past-tense transitive verb 'threw' or 'cast off' (here 'tore off/threw down'). یک ران و یک پای اوی lists the affected body parts: یک ران 'one thigh' and یک پای 'one foot' with اوی again the third‑person possessive 'of him/his'. The verb takes these as its object: 'he tore off one thigh and one foot of him.'
segments.s117-g53: بریده is a perfect/passive participial adjective 'cut off' or 'severed' describing what follows. برآویخت is a past verb meaning 'rose up' or 'sprang up.' با او به هم is a short prepositional phrase 'against him together' (با او = with/at him, به هم = together/at one another). Grammatically this gives 'the severed [one] sprang up against him together.'
segments.s117-g54: چو is a comparative/simile 'like/as.' پیل سرافراز و شیر دژم is a compound simile 'an exalted/proud elephant and a grim lion' (پیل = elephant, سرافراز = lofty/proud/triumpant; شیر = lion, دژم = sullen/grim). The clause modifies the preceding action, comparing the force of the springing to these two powerful beasts.
segments.s117-g55: همی is a frequentative/continuous adverb 'kept/continually' or an emphatic particle. پوست کند is a verb phrase 'peels/strips the skin' (پوست = skin, کند = does/peels). این از آن آن ازین is a chiasmic phrasing 'this from that, that from this' indicating mutual tearing. The whole segment describes repeated flaying: 'they continually stripped skin — this from that, that from this.'
segments.s117-g56: همی again marks continuous action 'so that' / 'continually' (here linking result). گل شد is an idiomatic/cataphoric phrase 'became a flower' used metaphorically (گل = flower, شد = became) to mean the ground was turned into a spectacle of red; از خون سراسر زمین is a prepositional phrase 'from blood throughout the earth' (از = from/of, خون = blood, سراسر = throughout/entire, زمین = earth/ground). Together: 'the whole ground became as a flower of blood' i.e. was covered with blood.
segments.s117-g73: همه غار یکسر پر از کشته بود: here the phrase-order places determiner/adverb before the noun: همه غار = 'the whole/entire cave'. یکسر is an adverb modifying the following predicate to mean 'entirely' or 'utterly'. پر از + noun is a fixed adjective phrase meaning 'full of ...' and بود is the past copula 'was'. The line literally reads, 'the whole cave was entirely full of slain (people)'.
segments.s117-g74: جهان همچو دریای خون گشته بود: همچو (classical/poetic 'like') introduces a simile; دریای خون is a genitive (construct) 'sea of blood'. گشته بود is a compound past ('had become' / 'had turned into'), with بود as the auxiliary past. Order is subject — simile phrase — verb (perfective result).
segments.s117-g75: بیامد ز اولاد بگشاد بند: بیامد is the past 'came'; ز اولاد is a source phrase 'from (among) the offspring/sons'. بگشاد here functions as a verb/participial notion 'opened/unfastened' and takes بند 'fetter/bond' as its object. Subject is omitted (typical epic terseness) and is understood from context (one of the sons).
segments.s117-g76: به فتراک بربست پیچان کمند: بربست is the past transitive 'he bound/fastened'. به + noun (به فتراک) marks the goal/instrumental location 'onto the cloak/saddle-cloth'. پیچان modifies کمند and precedes it (poetic adjective order): 'a winding/twisting snare'. The sequence is verb after the to-phrase, typical of Persian narrative.
segments.s117-g77: به اولاد داد آن کشیده جگر: به اولاد is the indirect-object phrase 'to the sons'. داد is the past 'gave'. آن is the demonstrative 'that' modifying کشیده جگر, where کشیده here is a participial/adjectival form 'drawn/pulled out' describing جگر (the organ). Word order is indirect object — verb — direct object phrase.
segments.s117-g78: سوی شاه کاووس بنهاد سر: سوی marks direction/toward 'to/in the presence of the king'. شاه کاووس is the addressee/recipient in whose presence the action occurs. بنهاد (past of نهادن) is a past verb 'placed/put' and سر is the direct object 'head'. The order is directional phrase — addressee — verb — object.
segments.s117-g79: بدو گفت اولاد کای نره شیر: بدو is an old-fashioned dative 'to him'. گفت here functions as a narrative marker 'said' followed by the speaker (اولاد = 'the sons'). کای is a vocative 'O', and نره شیر is a compound epithet 'manly lion' (نره qualifying شیر). The phrase is a direct speech introduction.
segments.s117-g80: جهانی به تیغ آوریدی به زیر: آوریدی is the 2nd-person past 'you brought' (or 'you have brought'). جهانی here refers to 'the world' (whole/universal). به تیغ uses به in an instrumental sense 'by/with the sword' and به زیر is a prepositional phrase 'to/into under' meaning 'under (someone's) control' or figuratively 'to the ground' — together: 'you have brought the world under (the sword)'. Word order places instrument and final prepositional phrase after the verb.
segments.s117-g1: وزان جایگه تنگ بسته کمر is a compact prepositional + noun phrase. وزان (a poetic form: و + زان) means “and/from that (place).” جایگه is the noun “place.” تنگ بسته کمر is an adjectival phrase meaning “waist tightly bound” where تنگ (tight/close) and بسته (bound/tied) together modify کمر (waist); adjective order is inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s117-g2: بیامد پر از کینه و جنگ سر is the main verb clause. بیامد is past “he came.” پر از = “full of” (پر + preposition از). کینه و جنگ are the contents “hatred and war,” and سر (literally “head”) is used idiomatically: “his head/full mind was full of hatred and war” (i.e. he was filled with hatred and the urge to fight).
segments.s117-g3: چو رخش اندر آمد بران هفت کوه begins with چو “when.” رخش is the name of the steed. اندر آمد = “entered/came in” (اندر = in/within + آمد = came). بران هفت کوه = “on/thereupon those seven mountains,” where بران is a locative/poetic form meaning “on/thereon” and هفت کوه = “seven mountains.”
segments.s117-g4: بران نره دیوان گشته گروه is a verbless descriptive clause: بران again gives location “thereon,” نره دیوان = “male (nare) demons” (نره modifies دیوان), and گشته گروه (گشته = become/turned; گروه = band/troop) means “have become a troop.” The participle گشته + noun expresses result/state (“have been formed into a group”).
segments.s117-g5: به نزدیکی غار بی‌بن رسید is a location + motion clause. به نزدیکی = “to/near the vicinity (of),” غار بی‌بن = “the cave of the rootless” (بی‌بن an epithet meaning ‘rootless’/‘without lineage’ modifying غار), and رسید is the past “he reached/arrived.”
segments.s117-g6: به گرد اندرون لشکر دیو دید is the observation clause. به گرد اندرون = “around/at the circumference of the interior” (به + گرد + اندرون giving a locative adverbial), لشکر دیو = “an army/host of demons,” and دید is the past verb “he saw.” The whole phrase says he beheld a demon-host around the inner part.
segments.s117-g7: به اولاد گفت آنچ پرسیدمت is a reported-speech clause. به اولاد = “to (his) sons,” گفت is the past “said” (here introducing the content), آنچ = “that which/what,” and پرسیدمت is an archaic past form “I asked you” (پرسید + enclitic ـت object ‘you’ + ـم ‘I’), giving “that which I had asked you.”
segments.s117-g8: همه بر ره راستی دیدمت is a short clause giving the speaker’s observation. همه = “entirely/altogether,” بر ره راستی = “on the road/way of truth/uprightness” (بر = on/upon, ره = way, راستی = truth/uprightness), and دیدمت = “I saw you” (past with enclitic object ـت = you). The adverbial phrase modifies دیدمت: “I saw you wholly on the right path.”
segments.s117-g25: میان سپاه اندر آمد چو گرد is a short narrative clause: میان سپاه اندر آمد = 'he came into the middle of the host' (میان = 'middle/in the midst of' + سپاه 'the host' + اندر آمد = 'entered/ came in'). چو گرد is a simile 'like a گرد'; چو functions as 'like/as' and گرد here names the dust‑whirl/whirlwind image. Word order is typical classical Persian: adverbial/locative (میان سپاه) before the verb آمد, then the simile چو + noun.
segments.s117-g26: سران را سر از تن همی دور کرد is a transitive clause: سران را marks 'the chiefs' as definite direct object (را). سر از تن دور کردن is a causative/active phrase 'to remove the head from the body'; همی before the verb کرد is an archaic narrative particle indicating continuous or habitual past action (classical usage, here part of the past narrative). کرد is the main past-tense verb 'made/did'.
segments.s117-g27: ناستاد کس پیش او در به جنگ is built with an initial negative verb ناستاد = 'did not stand' (classical negative past). کس here, in combination with the negative verb, reads as 'no one'. پیش او = 'before him/in his presence' and در به جنگ = 'in battle' (در as locative + به indicating the event). The clause order places the negated verb first for emphasis, followed by the subject and locatives.
segments.s117-g28: نجستند با او یکی نام و ننگ uses نجستند (from جستن) in the sense 'strove/contended' (here 'made an effort/struggled'), with با او 'with him' as the object of contest. یکی before the coordinated nouns نام و ننگ works like 'either/one (of the two)' or simply 'one' linking the pair 'name and shame'—here meaning 'for name (reputation) or disgrace'. The conjunction و joins the two nouns.
segments.s117-g29: رهش باز دادند و بگریختند: رهش باز دادند is an idiom meaning 'they gave (him) a way/escape' or 'they opened a path (for him)' (رهش = release/way, باز = 'open/again' used adverbially, دادند = 'they gave/made'). The conjunction و links the following verb بگریختند 'they fled/ran away' (simple past plural).
segments.s117-g30: به آورد با او نیاویختند is a negative past: نیاویختند is the negative past of آویختن (to cling/come to grips), so 'they did not grapple/engage (with) him'. با او 'with him' is the complement; به آورد ('brought to' literally) here is a light verbal/adverbial phrase introducing the attempt or effort and links to the negated verb—overall the clause means they made no attempt to attach/engage with him.
segments.s117-g31: وزان جایگه سوی دیو سپید begins with وزان ('and/from that') indicating 'from that place/time', جایگه 'place' names the source, followed by سوی 'toward' and دیو سپید 'the white demon' as the direction/target. Classical Persian often places the source phrase before the motion verb, which appears in the next segment.
segments.s117-g32: بیامد به کردار تابنده شید: بیامد is the past 'came'. به کردار is an instrumental/locative phrase 'in the manner/semblance of' (کردار 'manner/guise' used adverbially). تابنده 'shining/radiant' modifies the final noun شید; the sequence describes his appearance 'radiant as a شید'. This is a descriptive verbless simile attached to the motion verb.
segments.s117-g81: نشانهای بند تو دارد تنم is a compact nominal clause where نشانه‌ها (marks/traces) is the subject, بندِ تو (your fetter) modifies those marks, دارد is the present verb 'has' attaching to تنم (my body) as possessed/affected. Word order is topical/poetic: 'The marks of your fetter my body has' (i.e. 'my body bears the marks of your fetter').
segments.s117-g82: به زیر کمند تو بد گردنم is a verbless predicate clause describing the state of the speaker's neck. به زیر + زِیر = 'beneath/under' governs کمندِ تو ('your snare'), and بد گردنم is a predicative phrase 'my neck is bad/ill' (بد as predicative adjective; گردن‑م is the possessed noun 'my neck'). The clause is linked to the previous verb (دارد) by context but stands as a separate descriptive unit in the line.
segments.s117-g83: به چیزی که دادی دلم را امید is a relative construction: به چیزی که دادی = 'for/with the thing that you gave', where که دادی ('that you gave') modifies چیزی. دلم را امید literally puts دل ('heart') with the possessive/short form (دلم) and the object marker را before امید ('hope'), so the phrase reads as 'you (or it) gave my heart hope' / 'my heart has hope (because of) the thing you gave'. The order reflects poetic compression (possessive and object marker split across words).
segments.s117-g84: همی باز خواهد امیدم نوید is a future-oriented clause: همی باز = 'again/still', خواهد is the future/auxiliary 'will', امیدم 'my hope' is the subject/possessed noun, and نوید is the accusative/nominal complement ('tidings/good news' or 'promise'). Word order is verb (aux) then subject then object (a common flexible pattern in poetry): 'Again my hope will give/bring tidings.'
segments.s117-g85: به پیمان شکستن نه اندر خوری is an admonition built on a verbal noun: به پیمان شکستن = 'in/with respect to breaking the pact' (شکستن is the infinitive/gerund 'breaking'), نه is negation 'do not', and اندر خوری is an idiomatic verbal phrase (اندر + خوری) meaning roughly 'to act thus' or 'to let it befall/come to pass in this way'. The whole segment means 'Do not (let) the breaking of the covenant happen in this way.'
segments.s117-g86: که شیر ژیانی و کی منظری uses که as a causal/explanatory 'for/because' connecting the warning to the addressee's status. شیر ژیانی and کی منظری are names/titles presented in apposition (joined by و), so the clause says 'for you are Shir Zhiyānī and Kay Manzari' (i.e. persons of standing). In other words, the speaker forbids covenant‑breaking because of the addressee's rank/names.
segments.s117-g87: بدو گفت رستم که مازندران is inverted poetic word order: بدو ('to him') + گفت ('said') + رستم (Rostam) — literally 'to him said Rostam' — followed by the complementizer که introducing Rostam's speech/content (مازندران ...). This is a common classical sequence: indirect speech marker (بدو گفت) + speaker + که + reported clause.
segments.s117-g88: سپارم ترا از کران تا کران is the reported clause: سپارم is first‑person present/subjunctive or prospective 'I entrust/commit', ترا (ت + را written together) is the object 'you' + object marker ra, and از کران تا کران is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'from edge to edge' = 'from end to end'. The whole line is 'I entrust Mazandaran to you, from one end to the other.'
segments.s117-g41: An adverbial locative phrase followed by the object and then the verb: 'به تاریکی اندر' = 'in/into the darkness' (prepositional phrase), 'یکی کوه' = 'a certain/a mountain' (indefinite object), and 'دید' is the past tense verb 'he saw'. Persian here follows a typical SOV arrangement with the verb at the end and the subject omitted (=he/it).
segments.s117-g42: 'سراسر شده' is a perfective/resultative phrase meaning 'had become entire/covered', followed by 'غار' (the cave) and 'ازو ناپدید' literally 'disappeared from him/it' — idiomatically 'became invisible/hidden to him'. The auxiliary/participial 'شده' + adjective 'سراسر' yields 'was wholly/entirely so'.
segments.s117-g43: A descriptive clause introduced by 'به' functioning adverbially ('in/with the color'): 'به رنگ شبه روی' = 'with a hue/colour like a semblance-face' (i.e. ghostlike/pale complexion). 'و چون شیر موی' uses 'چون' for a simile: 'and hair like a lion's' (noun-noun 'شیر موی' = lion's mane). Word order places the descriptive phrases before the coming clause.
segments.s117-g44: A nominal result clause: 'جهان پر ز پهنای و بالای اوی' = 'the world [was] full from his breadth and his height'. 'پر ز' = 'full of/filled by', where 'ز' marks the source/material ('from/with') and 'اوی' is the old third‑person pronoun 'his'. The copula is omitted, giving a stative description.
segments.s117-g45: 'سوی رستم آمد' = 'he came toward Rostam' (سوی + proper name = toward), followed by the simile marker 'چو' + 'کوهی سیاه' = 'like a black mountain'. 'آمد' is simple past 'came'; the simile 'چو کوهی سیاه' modifies the manner of approach.
segments.s117-g46: Two verbless nominal/material clauses compressing 'was': 'از آهنش ساعد' = 'his forearm [was] of iron' and 'ز آهن کلاه' = 'and his helmet [was] of iron'. The preposition 'از/ز' marks material ('made of/from iron'), and the possessive suffix '-ش' on 'آهن' links the iron to him.
segments.s117-g47: 'ازو' here means 'because of him' or 'on account of him'. 'شد' is the past of 'to become' and takes the nominal predicate 'دل پیلتن پرنهیب' = 'the heart of Piltan [became] پرنهیب'. 'پیلتن' is a proper/place name in this line and 'پرنهیب' is an adjective meaning 'formidable/filled with dread' — so the whole phrase means 'Piltan's heart became filled with dread/was stricken.'
segments.s117-g48: 'بترسید' is third‑person past ('they/he feared' or 'was afraid'). 'کامد' is an archaic/past form 'came/arrived' (here narratively 'turned/moved'). 'به تنگی نشیب' is a destination phrase: 'به' = 'to', 'تنگی' = 'narrowness' (pass), and 'نشیب' = 'descent' — together 'to the narrow descent/pass'. The clause narrates that, in fear, they moved to the narrow descent.
segments.s117-g65: همی + past verb (گفت) is a poetic/progressive past construction meaning 'kept saying' or 'was saying'. ازین گونه is an older orthography/word-order for این‌گونه ('in this manner'); دیو سپید (demon + adjective) is the late‑positioned subject, typical of Persian poetic word order.
segments.s117-g66: همی here again gives a continuous/emphatic coloring to the past verb داد ('gave'). دل را is a marked direct object (را), 'his heart' as the thing affected; بدینسان نوید is the noun phrase 'such/this promise/assurance' placed after the verb for emphasis (verb–object inversion typical in poetry).
segments.s117-g67: تهمتن is the subject (proper name/epithet). به + noun (نیروی جان‌آفرین) marks the means/instrument ('by/with the life‑giving force'); جان‌آفرین is a compound adjective 'life‑giving' modifying نیروی. The prepositional phrase precedes the verb for emphasis.
segments.s117-g68: بکوشید is past tense, 3rd person singular of کوشیدن 'to strive/try' ('he strove'). بسیار is an adverb ('greatly, mightily') modifying بکوشید. با + (درد و کین) indicates accompaniment/manner ('with pain and vengeance'); و simply links the two nouns.
segments.s117-g69: بزد (past 'he struck') + دست ('hand') is a compact action clause 'he struck with his hand'. برداشتش is برداشت + enclitic -ش (object pronoun 'it/him'), here 'he lifted/took it up.' نره شیر is a post‑posed noun phrase 'the male lion' (نره modifies شیر).
segments.s117-g70: به گردن برآورد is a verbal phrase: به گردن = 'to/on the neck' and برآورد here functions as a past tense verb 'he raised/put (it) to the neck' (poetic usage). و افگند زیر is 'and threw/cast it under/beneath' (افگند = he threw; زیر = under).
segments.s117-g71: فرو (adverbial prefix/adverb) + برد here forms a compound indicating a downward thrust ('he thrust in'). خنجر is the direct object 'dagger'. دلش is دل + enclitic ش ('his heart'), and بردرید (past) means 'was/was torn apart' or 'tore apart' — here describing the heart being ripped.
segments.s117-g72: جگرش = 'his liver' (possessive enclitic ش). از تن تیره is a prepositional phrase 'out of the dark/dusky body' (از = out of, تن = body, تیره = dark/black). بیرون کشید is a compound verb 'drew/pulled out' (past).
segments.s117-g17: بدانگه (thereupon) is a temporal adverb placed before the clause. تو پیروز باشی uses باشی in a non-imperative present/subjunctive sense ('you be / you are') with پیروز as a predicative adjective; word order is subject (تو) + predicate (پیروز باشی), typical of classical Persian. مگر at the end of this segment is a limiting/contrastive particle that prepares a conditional qualification continued in the next segment.
segments.s117-g18: مگر اگر here functions together as a conditional limiter meaning 'but only if / provided that.' اگر introduces the condition; یار is the ally/companion, and باشدت is an archaic subjunctive/present form with the enclitic -ت ('may there be to you' / 'you may have') marking the indirect object; پیروزگر is an agent noun ('victor, one who makes victory'). The whole phrase reads 'if you have an ally who is a victor.'
segments.s117-g19: نکرد is the past negative of کردن ('did not do'); it precedes the emphatic negative adverb ایچ ('not at all') here, so the clause reads 'did not at all.' رستم is the subject placed after the negated verb (a common classical order). به رفتن شتاب is a prepositional phrase with the gerund رفتن ('to go') governed by به and the noun شتاب ('haste') — literally 'haste in going' = 'hurry to depart.'
segments.s117-g20: بدان تا is a fused temporal conjunction meaning 'until' (literally 'to that until'); تا reinforces the temporal subordination. برآمد is the past tense verb 'rose' and بلند آفتاب is the noun phrase 'the high/tall sun' (adjective before noun in this poetic order); together they mean 'until the sun rose high.'
segments.s117-g21: سراپای (sara-pā) is an adverbial/nominal phrase meaning 'from head to foot / entirely' and modifies the following noun اولاد ('children'), so 'the children's whole bodies.' بر هم is a fixed adverbial 'together' (بر + هم) and ببست is the past verb 'he bound/tied' (literally 'he bound together'). Word order places the object phrase before the verb as is common in poetry.
segments.s117-g22: به خم کمند is a prepositional phrase 'on/into the bend of the rope/lasso' where خم ('curve, bend') is linked to کمند ('lasso/snare') in a tight noun phrase (poetic word order). آنگهی is a temporal adverb 'then/thereupon' and برنشست is the past-tense verb 'he sat upon' (بر + نشست = 'took seat on').
segments.s117-g23: برآهیخت is a past intransitive verb 'rose up / sprang up.' جنگی is an adjective 'warlike' modifying the following noun نهنگ ('giant' here, a hyperbolic term), so the noun phrase 'a warlike giant.' از نیام is a prepositional phrase 'out of the scabbard' (from the sheath) indicating sudden emergence.
segments.s117-g24: بغرید is a past verb 'he roared/bellowed.' چون رعد is a simile 'like thunder.' و connects the clauses, and برگفت نام is a compound verbal phrase (بر + گفت) meaning 'he uttered/exclaimed a name' — برگفت here functions as 'spoke up/voiced,' followed by نام ('name').
segments.s117-g129: همه is the subject meaning “all/everyone.” برکشیدند is a past 3rd-person-plural verb (بر + کشیدن) meaning “they raised/drew” (here physical raising or drawing of weapons). گرز گران is the object: گرز (noun) “mace/club” followed by the adjective گران “heavy/weighty” in the normal post-nominal adjective position; the whole phrase thus: “they raised heavy maces.”
segments.s117-g130: پراگنده is an adjectival/adverbial word “scattered/widespread” describing location or distribution. در introduces the prepositional phrase “in/within,” شهر is “city,” and مازندران names the place (here the mythic/epic realm of Mazandaran). The phrase as a whole locates the action: “scattered in the city of Mazandaran.”
segments.s117-g131: برفتند is past 3rd-plural “they went/proceeded.” یکسر is an adverb here meaning “all at once/altogether.” به فرمان is the prepositional phrase “at/by the command (of)”; کی (an archaic royal name/title 'Kay') follows in apposition/genitive position to indicate whose command it was — a common epic/poetic construction where the possessor follows the thing commanded.
segments.s117-g132: چو is the comparative/simile marker “as/like.” آتش is the noun “fire.” که introduces a relative clause “that/which,” and برخیزد is a present/subjunctive 3rd-singular form of برخاستن “to rise/come up” used in the simile. از + خشک + نی = “from/out of a dry reed,” with خشک modifying نی. The line compares their movement to fire springing from dry reed.
segments.s117-g133: ز is the poetic preposition “from/on account of/with.” شمشیر تیز is the noun + adjective “sharp sword,” with the adjective after the noun. آتش is the object “fire,” and افروختند is past 3rd-plural “they kindled/ignited,” causatively producing the fire with their swords.
segments.s117-g134: همه شهر = “the whole city” (همه here = entire/whole modifying شهر). یکسر is an adverb “entirely/at once.” همی is an archaic/emphatic particle often marking continuous or emphatic action in classical Persian before the verb; سوختند is past 3rd-plural “they burned” (the inhabitants or the city itself as subject). Together: “the whole city burned up completely.”
segments.s117-g135: به لشکر is the prepositional phrase “to the army.” چنین is an adverb “thus/so,” and گفت is the past 3rd-singular verb “said.” کاووس شاه (Kavus the king) follows the verb in a verb–subject order typical of Persian poetic style, marking the speaker (subject) after the speech verb for emphasis.
segments.s117-g136: که is the conjunction “that.” اکنون is “now/at present.” مکافات is the noun “retribution/punishment” acting as the predicate subject, کرده is the past participle “done/has been done” (forming a perfect/resultative sense with مکافات), and گناه is the object “sin.” The clause therefore means “that now retribution has been exacted upon sin” (i.e., sin has been punished).
segments.s117-g121: نشست is a past‑tense verb meaning 'sat' (third person plural or singular depending on context); از بر تخت مازندران is a prepositional phrase meaning 'upon the throne of Mazandaran' where از + بر is an archaic/prepositional stacking emphasizing location 'on/upon', تخت = 'throne', and مازندران names the realm.
segments.s117-g122: This segment lists the occupants. ابا here does not match the dictionary senses given (it is not functioning as 'refuse' here) and appears as an archaic/epithetical element before the name; رستم is the proper name 'Rostam', و = 'and', نامور = an adjective 'renowned', مهتران = plural noun 'chiefs/elders'. Together they form the subject phrase 'Rostam and the renowned chiefs'.
segments.s117-g123: چو introduces a list meaning 'such as' / 'like', followed by a sequence of proper names in coordination connected by و ('and'). Each name is a nominative list item; the whole segment simply enumerates heroic personages.
segments.s117-g124: A continuation of the list introduced by چو: چو = 'and likewise/such as', then three more names joined by و. The final token نیو is an epithet/appendage attached to فرهاد; here it likely functions as a nickname or epithet (possibly 'Neyu' / 'ney (reed‑flute)') rather than the adjective 'new'.
segments.s117-g125: برین گونه = بر + این + گونه = 'in this manner' (an adverbial phrase). یک هفته = 'for one week' (duration). با رود و می = 'with river(s) and wine' (با + noun phrase; رود = 'river', می = 'wine'). The whole segment sets the temporal and instrumental circumstances of the action that follows.
segments.s117-g126: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle meaning 'continually' or 'kept on'; رامش = 'tranquility/peace' or the name of the peaceful festivity; آراست is a past tense verb 'adorned/arrayed' (he/they adorned); کاووس is the agent/subject 'Kavus' placed after the verb (typical word order in poetry), and کی is a relative/introducing particle here meaning 'who'. Literally: 'who Kavus continually adorned the repose' → naturally: 'Kavus kept adorning the festivity.'
segments.s117-g127: به هشتم = 'on the eighth (day)' (به + ordinal); نشستند is the past plural verb 'they sat' (the dictionary lemma given is mismatched — here it is the regular past of نشستن); بر زین = 'upon the saddle' (بر + زین), and همه = 'all', which introduces the list of people who sat. Word order places the temporal adverbial first, verb next, then location phrase and the quantified subject.
segments.s117-g128: A tripartite apposition listing those who sat: جهانجوی = 'world‑seeker(s)' (ambitious ones), و = 'and', گردنکشان = 'those who pull necks' i.e. proud/rebellious warriors, و = 'and', رمه = 'flock/multitude' (used for the throng). Each noun is coordinated; together they characterize the assembled crowd.
segments.s117-g57: به دل گفت is a fixed word order meaning 'said to (his) heart' (i.e., he spoke to himself). رستم‌گر here is an address/insert (vocative or exclamatory epithet invoking Rostam) placed after the verb phrase; امروز جان is the beginning of the conditional clause, 'today (my) life' with the possessive understood from context.
segments.s117-g58: بماند is a subjunctive/optative 3rd‑person form ('may/remain' or 'if ... remains') introducing the protasis of a conditional; به من marks the dative ('for me' / 'to me'); زنده‌ام is the 1st‑person present ('I am alive') with the enclitic ام marking the subject; جاودان follows as an adjectival predicate ('eternal'), giving the result clause sense: if life remains, I will be alive eternally.
segments.s117-g59: همیدون is a proper name (the speaker). به دل گفت means 'said to his heart' (i.e., he spoke inwardly). گفت followed by دیو سپید (post‑verbal) yields 'said, the white demon' — Persian often places the speaker after the verb in reported speech, so دیو سپید is the agent designation 'the white demon'.
segments.s117-g60: که introduces the content clause ('that ...'). از in this context means 'about/with respect to' (of the object of despair). جان شیرین is a genitive phrase 'sweet/precious life'; شدم is the past 1st‑person of شدن 'I became', and ناامید is the resulting adjectival predicate 'hopeless/despairing'.
segments.s117-g61: گر is the conditional conjunction 'if'. ایدونک is an archaic/uncertain adverbial particle here adding sense of chance or 'by some means/ perchance'. از چنگ این اژدها is a prepositional phrase 'from the claw/grip of this dragon' with از marking separation and چنگ 'claw/grip' governing the genitive construction.
segments.s117-g62: بریده is a past participial/adjectival form 'cut off/torn'; پی و پوست is a coordinated noun phrase 'flesh (پی) and skin (پوست)' (poetic word order placing the modifiers after the participle). یابم is a 1st‑person subjunctive/present form 'I may/find' used as the main verb of the result clause, and رها is an adjectival/resultive complement 'free/released' that describes the state obtained.
segments.s117-g63: نه ... نه ... is the correlative 'neither ... nor ...'. کهتر 'inferior' and برتر 'superior' are the two poles being denied; منش ('stature/character/origin') governs what is being compared, and مهتران (here the plural of مهتر, 'elders/chiefs') supplies the reference group — a possessive/genitive relation (منشِ مهتران) is implied by the word order and elliptical grammar common in poetry.
segments.s117-g64: نبینند is a negative present/subjunctive form 'they do not/shall not see' with the subject omitted (third plural). نیزم is نیـز 'also' with the first‑person enclitic ـم attached, giving 'me also' / 'me either' as the object; به مازندران locates the action 'in/at Mazandaran'. Together: 'they will not see me in Mazandaran either.'
segments.s117-g137: This is a temporal/causal clause: چنان چون ... رسید = “when/inasmuch as such ... reached (them).” چنان (so/thus) pairs with چون (as/when) in a poetic correlative; سزا بد = “bad/terrible punishment” (noun + adjective, adjective after noun as usual); بدیشان is the enclitic compound (به + ایشان) meaning “to/for them”; رسید is simple past ‘reached / befell.’
segments.s117-g138: ز کشتن = “from/with respect to killing” (ز is the poetic variant of از). کنون = “now.” دست... کشید is the idiom دست کشیدن (از X) meaning “to withdraw one’s hand from / to desist from X”; here دست is the literal noun “hand” used in the idiom. باید is the modal ‘must/ought.’ کشید is the verb ‘to draw/withdraw’ in poetic/past form attached to باید to express necessity (“one must withdraw”); the object (از کشتن) precedes the idiom.
segments.s117-g139: بباید is a classical/modal form equivalent to باید (“it is necessary / there ought to be”). یکی مرد = “one man / a single man” (یکی here = someone / a certain). با + qualities (هوش و سنگ) lists his attributes: با هوش = “with intellect / clever,” و سنگ = “and stone” (stone = figuratively steady/firm). The sequence presents a required person: “there ought to be a man, intelligent and firm.”
segments.s117-g140: کجا introduces the content of knowing: “where.” باز here functions adverbially (poetic) — “clearly / openly / properly” — modifying داند. داند is present simple “knows.” شتاب از درنگ places two opposites in contrast: شتاب = “haste,” درنگ = “delay/pausing,” with از marking the separation/comparison (“haste from delay” = where to prefer/curb haste as opposed to delay). Together: “who knows where (properly) haste should be distinguished from delay / when to hasten and when to wait.”
segments.s117-g141: شود is third-person present/future or modal “will be / will go” (classical use of شدن to indicate motion/change). نزد = “in the presence of / to” (with a person). سالار = “commander / lord” (title). مازندران is the place/name. Together: “he will be/come to the commander of Mazandaran.”
segments.s117-g142: کند here is causative/basic verb “makes/does,” so کند دلش بیدار = “he makes his heart awake” (i.e. his heart becomes alert). The suffix ش attached to دل and مغز is the third-person possessive “his.” مغزش گران = “his brain/mind heavy,” where گران (“heavy”) is used metaphorically to mean grave/serious or weighty in thought. Adjectives follow their nouns (typical Persian order).
segments.s117-g143: بران کار = “on that matter / concerning that affair” (بران is a poetic adverbial = ‘thereon’ / ‘on that’). خشنود شد = past “became pleased / was pleased.” پور زال is the name/title “Pour (son) Zal” (subject of the clause, placed after the verb in poetic order).
segments.s117-g144: بزرگان = “the great ones / nobles.” The relative clause که بودند با او همال = “who were with him as companions/attendants.” که introduces the relative clause; بودند is past plural ‘they were’ agreeing with بزرگان; با او = ‘with him’; همال = attendants/carriers/companions (used here of his associates).
segments.s117-g97: ازان پس (lit. 'from that then') is an archaic compound meaning 'thereafter' or 'from that time on'. اگر introduces a conditional; بسپرم is the first‑person present/subjunctive form of سپردن (to entrust/hand over) used here to present the alternative 'if I hand over...'. خاک را uses the object marker را to mark 'the land/soil' as the definite direct object.
segments.s117-g98: وگرنه functions as 'or else'/'otherwise' linking the alternative. ز is the poetic form of از (here meaning 'from/of') and introduces the object پیمان تو 'your covenant'. نگذرم is ن + گذرم (present/subjunctive/negative first person) meaning 'I will not pass/abandon' — here idiomatically 'I will not break/abandon (my) covenant'.
segments.s117-g99: رسید is the simple past 'he arrived/reached'. آنگهی is an older/poetic adverb meaning 'then' or 'thereupon'. نزد + proper name means 'to the presence of' or 'before' someone. کی here is the relative/identifying particle 'who' linking the following appositive phrase that describes the person reached.
segments.s117-g100: This is a string of noun + epithets describing the person: یل 'hero' followed by پهلو افروز (a compound epithet literally 'side‑illuminating' = Pahlu‑afroz) and فرخنده پی ('fortunate/auspicious‑footed'). Poetic Persian commonly stacks such epithets after the noun; پی here functions as 'foot/‑footed' forming an epithet.
segments.s117-g101: چنین گفت is the formula 'thus he said'. کای is the vocative 'O Kay' (an old royal vocative), here addressing the king. شاه دانش پذیر is a noun + adjective: 'king receptive to knowledge' (دانش پَذیر = 'knowledge‑accepting'). In classical Persian adjectives often follow the noun they modify.
segments.s117-g102: This line strings two adjectival/imperative‑like phrases aimed at the king. به مرگ بداندیش literally 'with regard to death, ill‑thinking' — بداندیش is an adjective 'ill‑thinking' or 'of bad thought' applied to the king's attitude toward death (here best rendered as 'be wary/regard death with caution'). رامش پذیر is another adjective 'receptive to repose/peace' (رامش‑پذیر). Classical poetic usage often places such adjectives in vocative speech to urge or characterize behaviour.
segments.s117-g103: دریدم is the past tense 'I tore' (دریدن). جگرگاه is a compound noun meaning the stomach/epigastrium or figuratively 'innermost (seat of the liver/heart)'. دیو سپید is 'the white demon' (سپید modifying دیو). The verb precedes its object here, a common poetic word order.
segments.s117-g104: ندارد is 'does not have' (present tense of داشتن used here existentially). بدو = به او 'of/for him'. شاه is the subject 'the king' (poetic inversion places the verb before the subject). ازین پس is the temporal adverb 'from this time on' (از + این + پس). امید is 'hope' — together 'the king has no hope of him from now on.'
segments.s117-g113: This miniature clause places the indirect object first (به رستم = 'to Rostam'), then a comment marker + verb (چنین گفت = 'thus said'), then the speaker's name (کاووس = 'Kavus'). The final word کی here functions as a question/vocative introducer that leads into the direct address that follows; the verb گفت is past tense. Word order is markedly poetic (preposition + object before the reporting verb and subject).
segments.s117-g114: که‌ای is the fused sequence که + ای (i.e. 'who art thou, O…') introducing a direct address. گرد here is an epithet/term of address (poetic; not one of the literal senses given) and is therefore marked below as unknown. و links two adjectives; فرزانه is an adjective 'wise' and نیک‌پی is another epithet 'well‑born'. The whole unit is a vocative phrase addressing the addressee with stacked epithets.
segments.s117-g115: این phrase combines two locative elements: به چشم من ('into/for my eye') and اندر ('in/within') with the participial-looking چکان ('dripping') modifying خون ('blood'). اوی is a possessive 'his', so the whole clause gives the protasis: 'if his blood drips into my eye'. The verb is implicit here; the aspect is habitual/idealized (dripping as a hypothetical event).
segments.s117-g116: مگر here functions as 'unless' (introducing the apodosis). باز 'again' precedes the verb بینم ('I may see' / 'I see'); the verb is first‑person and carries the enclitic ت (w3) as its 2nd‑person object (see/meet you). را is the definite object marker attached after the clitic, and نیز 'also' modifies the action; روی 'face' is the object noun. Word order shows the verb with its clitic object followed by the object marker and noun phrase.
segments.s117-g117: چو functions temporally as 'when'. به چشمش اندر ('into his eye') gives the location; کشیدند is the past tense plural 'they drew' (here 'drew/pulled' the blood into the eye) and خون is the object 'blood'. The clause is a past temporal clause describing a completed action.
segments.s117-g118: شد is the past of شدن 'became' and introduces the result clause. آن دیده 'that eye' is the subject, and تیره خورشیدگون are predicative adjectives: تیره 'dark' followed by خورشیدگون 'sun‑colored' (a poetic compound). The adjective sequence after the noun is normal in poetic description.
segments.s117-g119: نهادند is the past plural of نهادن 'they placed/set'. زیراندرش is a single fused locative adverb meaning 'beneath him/under him'. تخت عاج is the object phrase 'ivory throne' (تخت 'throne' qualified by عاج 'ivory'). The clause is a straightforward past transitive construction: verb + locative + object.
segments.s117-g120: بیاویختند is past plural 'they hung/suspended'. از بر عاج is a prepositional phrase 'on/over the ivory' (از بر used poetically to mark position on/over), and تاج is the object 'crown'. The two clauses describe successive ceremonial actions (place throne, hang the crown).
segments.s117-g105: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and governs the following noun phrase. پهلوش is پهلو + ـش (possessive suffix) = 'his flank/side'. بیرون is an adverb 'out/away' placed before the verb to mark outward motion. کشیدم is the past first-person singular 'I pulled/drew' (کشید + -م). جگر is the direct object 'liver' placed after the verb in this line for poetic word order, so the whole segment reads literally 'from his flank out I pulled the liver.'
segments.s117-g106: چه is the interrogative 'what'. فرمان is the noun 'command/order' (direct object). دهد is third-person singular present (or present-subjunctive/optative) of دادن used here to express 'will give' or 'does give' in a rhetorical question. شاه پیروزگر is the subject 'the victorious king' (شاه modified by the epithet پیروزگر). Note the typical Persian inversion: object (فرمان) + verb (دهد) + subject (شاه پیروزگر).
segments.s117-g107: برو is an imperative 'go' (here uttered as part of the acclamation). آفرین is an exclamation 'bravo' or 'well done' functioning as the content of the praise. کرد is the past tense 'he did/made' and with آفرین forms the verbal phrase آفرین کرد = 'he exclaimed/praised'. کاووس شاه is the postposed subject 'Kavus the king' (proper name + title). In Persian reported speech the quoted imperative/exclamation appears before the verb کرد.
segments.s117-g108: که introduces a subordinate clause giving the content of the king's words ('that ...'). بی‌تو is the prepositional phrase 'without you' (بی‑ + تو). مبادا is a negative optative/wish meaning 'may it not be' or 'let it not happen' (used to forbid or wish against something). نگین و کلاه are the coordinated nouns 'gem (jewel)' and 'crown/hat' — the things the king says should not exist without the addressee.
segments.s117-g109: بران here functions adverbially/prefatorily as 'therefore'/'by that' (literally 'on that'). مام is a dialectal/poetic form for 'mother'. کاو here stands with مام to indicate 'the mother of Kavus' (name element; the genitive relation is implicit). چون تو فرزند زاد = a verbless relative phrase 'a child born like you' (چون = 'like/as', تو = 'you', فرزند = 'son/child', زاد = birth/gave birth/was born). The sequence is nominal and describes the exceptional nature of such a birth.
segments.s117-g110: نشاید is an impersonal/formal negative 'it is not fitting' or 'should not be' (modal). جز = 'except'. از introduces the source 'from' or cause. آفرین کرد is a noun+verb sequence 'the saying/act of praise' (literally 'praise did') and یاد is 'remembrance/memory'. Together جز از آفرین کرد یاد = 'except from the memory/remembrance of that praise' — i.e. such a birth would not occur save by virtue of that acclamation.
segments.s117-g111: مرا here is the combination of the old short first-person pronoun م plus the object/topic marker را (so 'to/for me' or 'my' in sense 'my fortune'). بخت is the noun 'fortune/fate'. ازین هر دو = 'from these two' or 'because of both of these' (از + این + هر دو). فرخترست is a poetic fused form: فرختر + است, meaning 'is more auspicious/fortunate' (comparative + copula). The whole clause is a nominal-predicate statement 'My fortune, because of these two, is more fortunate.'
segments.s117-g112: که introduces the content/reason clause. پیل هژبر is a noun phrase 'the elephant Hojabr' (پیل = 'elephant', هژبر here a proper name or epithet). افگنم is a poetic verb form (here functioning as a jussive/subjunctive/first-person action 'that I may cast/bring down' or causatively 'that I overthrow'); in classical poetry such forms express wished-for or potential action. کهترست is an adjective 'is lesser/inferior' (here likely meaning 'a lesser thing' or 'not a great matter' — i.e. the feat is the lesser of two, hence my fortune is all the more blessed).
segments.s117-g89: ترا is the clitic object pronoun 'to you' (object marker + 2nd-person). زین here is a concrete/figurative direct object ('saddle' or reins, i.e. authority); سپس is an adverb 'hence/thereafter'; بی‌نیازی is a noun 'independence/self-sufficiency' functioning as the object complement. دهم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive-form verb 'I give' placed at the end (SOV order: indirect object/objects before the verb).
segments.s117-g90: به + مازندران marks the beneficiary/location ('to/for Mazandaran'); سرفرازی is a noun 'honor/glory' that is the thing being given; دهم repeats the verb 'I give'. The clause is another coordinate object of the same first-person verb (parallel structure: 'I give X, I give Y').
segments.s117-g91: یکی here is an indefinite 'a certain/one' modifying کار ('a certain task'); پیشست (= پیش است) is the verbal phrase 'lies before / is ahead' with the copular sense; و links the next noun phrase رنج دراز, where رنج 'hardship' is qualified postpositively by دراز 'long'. Word order is typical Classical Persian noun + adjective after it.
segments.s117-g92: که introduces an explanatory relative/subordinate clause ('which/for it'); the paired هم ... هم ... construction marks 'both ... and ...'; با before نشیب and فراز is the preposition 'with' (literally 'with descent' / 'with ascent') and است is the copula 'is'. The clause explains that the hardship contains both downward and upward phases.
segments.s117-g93: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle common in epic verse (here giving immediacy or occasionality). شاه مازندران را is the noun phrase 'the king of Mazandaran' with را marking him as the definite object of the following verb; ز گاه literally 'from time' (ز = 'from', گاه = 'time') functions adverbially as 'at times' or 'from his place/occasion'.
segments.s117-g94: بباید is the impersonal/modal 'it is necessary / one must'; followed by two verbal infinitives/masdars ربودن ('to snatch away') and فگندن ('to cast/throw'), which act as its complements. به چاه uses به + noun to indicate direction/target ('into a pit'). The sequence means 'one must snatch (him) away and throw (him) into a pit.'
segments.s117-g95: سر is 'head' (direct object), followed by the compound دیو جادو 'sorcerous/enchanted demon' functioning as the noun modified by هزاران هزار ('thousands upon thousands'), an intensive plural quantifier emphasizing great number. The noun phrase therefore means 'the heads of countless sorcerous demons.'
segments.s117-g96: بیفگند is the verbal stem 'to throw/cast' (here in imperative/optative narration) combined with باید 'must' (modal necessity); the order 'بیفگند باید' is a poetic inversion of 'باید بیفگند'. به خنجر uses به instrumentally ('with the dagger') and the final به زار is resultative/degreeal meaning 'into wailing' or 'to make them wretched/mourn' (زار here is a noun/verb-noun 'lament').

sentences.s117-s73: He sent a letter to his close one in order to inflame his dark soul.
sentences.s117-s17: He beheld a cave like the likeness of Hell; the demon's body vanished into the darkness.
sentences.s117-s18: For a time he remained in the grasp of the sword; there was no place for meeting nor any path of escape.
sentences.s117-s19: From that darkness the eye saw no place; for a time he rested upon that spot.
sentences.s117-s20: When he rubbed his lashes and washed his eyes, he looked briefly about in that dark place.
sentences.s117-s5: Now, when the time to depart for the heights comes, show me the way and disclose the secret.
sentences.s117-s6: The son said to him: when the sun becomes hot and the demon comes into sleep.
sentences.s117-s7: You will be victorious over them in battle; now you must pause for a while.
sentences.s117-s8: Among the divs you will not see even one sitting, except for a few guards among the sorcerers.
sentences.s117-s25: The living elephant sprang up in fury; someone struck his sharp sword into the middle.
sentences.s117-s26: By Rostam's strength, from above him he threw down one thigh and one foot of him.
sentences.s117-s27: The severed (or cut) [thing] leapt up against him together, like a proud elephant and a grim lion.
sentences.s117-s28: They kept tearing off the hides, this from that and that from this; the whole earth became reddened with blood.
sentences.s117-s37: The whole cave was entirely full of the slain; the world had turned into a sea of blood.
sentences.s117-s38: From among the sons came (one) and unfastened the fetter; he fastened a twisting snare to the cloak.
sentences.s117-s39: He gave that drawn-out liver to his sons; he placed the head before King Kavus.
sentences.s117-s40: To him the sons said, 'O manly lion, by the sword you have brought the world under (your control).'
sentences.s117-s1: And from that place, with his waist tightly bound, he came, his head full of hatred and of war.
sentences.s117-s2: When Rakhsh entered, on those seven mountains the male demons had formed into a band.
sentences.s117-s3: He came near the cave of the rootless and, around the inner part, he saw a host of demons.
sentences.s117-s4: He said to his sons what I had asked you; I saw you wholly on the straight path.
sentences.s117-s13: He entered into the midst of the host like a whirlwind, and he severed the chiefs' heads from their bodies.
sentences.s117-s14: No one stood before him in battle; none contested with him for name or honor.
sentences.s117-s15: They gave him room and fled; they did not grapple with him.
sentences.s117-s16: From that place toward the white demon came one, radiant like a shid.
sentences.s117-s41: My body bears the marks of your fetter; my neck is ill beneath your snare.
sentences.s117-s42: For the thing you gave my heart has hope; my hope will again bring good news.
sentences.s117-s43: Do not break the covenant in such a way, for you are Shir Zhiyānī and Kay Manzari.
sentences.s117-s44: Rostam said to him, 'I will entrust Mazandaran to you from end to end.'
sentences.s117-s21: In the darkness he saw a certain mountain; the cave had become completely hidden from him.
sentences.s117-s22: He had a ghostlike hue to his face and hair like a lion's; his breadth and stature filled the world.
sentences.s117-s23: He came toward Rostam like a black mountain; his forearm and his helmet were of iron.
sentences.s117-s24: Because of him the heart of Piltan became full of dread; (they) were frightened and came to the narrow descent.
sentences.s117-s33: Thus the white demon kept saying, and gave his heart this assurance.
sentences.s117-s34: Tahmatan strove mightily with life‑giving force, in great pain and with vengeance.
sentences.s117-s35: He struck with his hand and lifted the male lion; he brought it up to the neck and cast it beneath.
sentences.s117-s36: He thrust the dagger in; his heart was torn, and he drew his liver out from his dark body.
sentences.s117-s9: At that moment you will be victorious — but only if you have an ally who makes you victorious.
sentences.s117-s10: Rostam did not hurry to go at all; he waited until the high sun rose.
sentences.s117-s11: He bound the children all over together, and then sat upon the loop of the lariat.
sentences.s117-s12: A warlike giant leapt up from the scabbard; he roared like thunder and cried out his name.
sentences.s117-s65: They all raised heavy maces, scattered throughout the city of Mazandaran.
sentences.s117-s66: They went all together at the command of Kay, like fire that rises from a dry reed.
sentences.s117-s67: They kindled fire with sharp swords; the whole city burned completely.
sentences.s117-s68: Kaavus the king said thus to the army: that now sin has received its punishment.
sentences.s117-s61: Upon the throne of Mazandaran sat Rostam and the famed chiefs.
sentences.s117-s62: Such were Tus and Fariburz and Goudarz and Giv; and Roham and Gorgin and Farhad (Neyu).
sentences.s117-s63: In this manner, for a week, with rivers and wine, Kavus kept decking the festivity.
sentences.s117-s64: On the eighth (day) they sat upon that saddle — all the ambitious, the neck‑slayers, and the throng.
sentences.s117-s29: He said to his heart, "O Rostam — if my life is preserved today, I shall remain alive forever."
sentences.s117-s30: The white demon said inwardly, 'That I have become despairing of my sweet life.'
sentences.s117-s31: If, by any chance, I can be cut free from the grasp of this dragon and find my flesh and skin loosened (i.e. freed), I will be released.
sentences.s117-s32: Neither lesser nor greater in stature (than the chiefs) — they will not see me in Mazandaran either.
sentences.s117-s69: Since such a terrible punishment befell them, one must now withdraw (one's) hand from killing.
sentences.s117-s70: There should be one man, intelligent and steadfast, who knows where to check haste and where to delay.
sentences.s117-s71: He will go to the lord (commander) of Mazandaran; his heart will be made alert and his mind become weighty.
sentences.s117-s72: Upon that matter Pour‑Zal was pleased; the great men who were with him were his companions/attendants.
sentences.s117-s49: Thereafter, whether I surrender the land or not, I will not abandon my pledge to you.
sentences.s117-s50: Then he came to Kay Kavus — the hero Pahlu‑afroz, fortunate of gait.
sentences.s117-s51: Thus he said: 'O king, be receptive to knowledge; be wary in the face of death; be open to repose.'
sentences.s117-s52: I tore open the white demon's liver‑chamber; the king has no hope of him from now on.
sentences.s117-s57: Kavus said to Rostam thus: "O — who are you, round one, wise and well-born!"
sentences.s117-s58: If his blood should drip into my eye — unless I may see your face again.
sentences.s117-s59: When they drew blood into his eye, that eye became dark, sun‑colored.
sentences.s117-s60: They placed an ivory throne beneath him and hung a crown above the ivory.
sentences.s117-s53: From his flank I pulled out the liver; what will the victorious king command?
sentences.s117-s54: Kavus the king cried, 'Go — bravo!' and (added), 'May there be no gem or crown without you.'
sentences.s117-s55: Therefore (or for that reason) the mother of Kavus could not have borne a child like you except through the remembrance of that praise.
sentences.s117-s56: My fortune is made happier by both of these things: that I may cast down the mighty elephant Hojabr — it is the lesser (task).
sentences.s117-s45: I will henceforth make you independent and I will grant you honor in Mazandaran.
sentences.s117-s46: A certain task lies ahead and a long hardship — it has both lows and highs.
sentences.s117-s47: At times one must snatch away the king of Mazandaran from his place and throw him into a pit.
sentences.s117-s48: He must, with the dagger, cast down the heads of countless sorcerous demons and bring them to wailing.
